planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; never collect from wild populations. Sweet Betsy is a sessile trillium — its maroon flowers sit directly on the three large, attractively mottled leaves without a stalk.

directions: Sweet Betsy trillium is one of the finest sessile trilliums in the Southeast, native to rich, moist deciduous forests from Virginia and Kentucky south to Georgia and Mississippi, where it carpets shaded forest floors with its dramatically silver-mottled leaves topped by stalkless dark maroon to reddish-purple flowers in March and April. The cuneate (wedge-shaped) base of the leaves distinguishes this species from the very similar Trillium sessile, and the flowers emit a faint, sweet fragrance in warm weather that attracts fly pollinators. Like all trilliums, Sweet Betsy requires 5–7 years to bloom from seed — always purchase plants from ethical nurseries propagating their own stock, and never collect from wild populations, which are protected in many states. Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil (pH 5.5–6.5) in partial to full shade; mulch lightly with leaf litter, keep consistently moist, and allow the foliage to yellow and die back naturally each summer.
sources: Flora of the Southeastern United States (Weakley)

planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, acidic to slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade. A coastal plain specialist, Spotted Trillium thrives in the warm, moist forests of Georgia, Alabama, and the Florida panhandle.

directions: Spotted Trillium is a sessile trillium of the southeastern coastal plain, native to moist, rich hardwood forests from South Carolina south to Florida and west to Alabama, where it often grows with sweetbay magnolia, titi, and evergreen shrubs in lowland forest understories. Its heavily marbled, silver-spotted leaves and nearly black maroon sessile flowers make it one of the more dramatic members of the genus, and the flowers carry a faint spicy scent that attracts fly pollinators. Rhizome divisions establish best when planted in fall in deeply amended, consistently moist, acidic soil (pH 5.0–6.0) in full shade; the plants go completely dormant by early summer and should never be disturbed while dormant. Source only from reputable nurseries growing their own stock — this species is never legitimately available from wild collection.

planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained acidic soil in partial to full shade; this rare species is restricted to the Savannah River drainage in Georgia, South Carolina, and adjacent North Carolina and should only be sourced from licensed nurseries propagating their own stock.

directions: Faded Trillium is a rare and geographically restricted sessile trillium endemic to the Savannah River drainage in the Piedmont and upper Coastal Plain of Georgia, South Carolina, and North Carolina, growing in moist, rich deciduous hardwood forests over well-drained, loamy soils. Its pale yellow to cream-colored sessile flowers are smaller and less showy than many trilliums — giving the species its common name — but the silvery-mottled leaves are attractive throughout the spring season. Classified as a species of conservation concern, this trillium should never be collected from wild populations; it is occasionally propagated by specialty native plant nurseries. Plant rhizome divisions in fall in deep, humus-rich, moist but well-drained acidic soil in the shade of deciduous trees, allowing the plants to emerge and bloom in spring before the canopy leafs out fully.
sources: Flora of the Southeastern United States (Weakley)

planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. Underwood's Trillium is a warm-climate sessile species suited to the moist hardwood and mixed pine-hardwood forests of the Florida panhandle, Georgia, and Alabama.

directions: Underwood's Trillium is a southeastern sessile trillium native to the moist, mixed hardwood forests of the Florida panhandle, Georgia, and Alabama, where it blooms in February through April with dark maroon to nearly black sessile flowers nestled among three strongly mottled, glossy leaves. It occupies an ecological niche similar to other coastal plain sessile trilliums but tolerates somewhat warmer and more humid conditions than its montane relatives, making it an appropriate choice for gardeners in zones 7–9 seeking native spring woodland plants. Obtain only nursery-propagated plants, as wild collection is destructive and in many areas illegal; plant rhizome divisions in fall in well-amended, moist, acidic soil in the shade of evergreen or deciduous trees. Like all trilliums, it blooms for only a few weeks before going dormant, so interplant with later-emerging shade perennials to fill the gap.

planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in deep, moist, rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in a woodland garden setting. Vasey's Trillium is a large pedicellate species that blooms somewhat later than sessile trilliums — April to May in the Southern Appalachians.

directions: Vasey's Trillium is one of the largest and most striking of the native eastern trilliums, native to rich, moist cove forests and sheltered ravines of the southern Appalachians from Virginia and North Carolina south to Georgia and Alabama, where it blooms from April through May with dramatic, deep wine-red petals that recurve strongly back toward the stem, hanging below the leaves and emitting a strong, sweet fragrance to attract fly pollinators. It grows markedly larger than most sessile trilliums, with broad three-parted leaves and stems reaching 24 inches, and its flowers hang on a distinct pedicel below the leaf whorl — a characteristic setting it apart from sessile species. Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in the deepest, most richly amended soil you can provide — cove forest conditions with high organic matter, consistent moisture, and full shade — and mulch heavily with leaf litter. Vasey's Trillium takes 5–7 years to reach blooming age from seed, making nursery divisions the only practical route for most gardeners.
sources: Flora of the Southeastern United States (Weakley)

planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade. Rugel's Trillium is a pedicellate species with strongly reflexed white petals that create a nodding appearance distinct from most other trilliums.

directions: Rugel's Trillium is a rare and distinctive pedicellate trillium native to a limited area in the southern Appalachians of Tennessee, North Carolina, and northern Georgia, where it grows in moist, rich hardwood forest coves and sheltered slopes and blooms in April with three white petals that reflex so strongly backward that they resemble a swept-back cap — an appearance unlike any other eastern trillium. The flower hangs on a short pedicel below the leaf whorl, facing downward and sometimes hidden beneath the foliage, and carries a faint fragrance that attracts small fly pollinators. Listed as a species of conservation concern in several states, Rugel's Trillium should never be collected from wild populations; source only from specialty native plant nurseries propagating their own rhizome divisions. Grow in deep, consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full shade, mulch generously with leaf litter, and allow the foliage to yellow and die back naturally by summer.

planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated transplants in early spring in moist, humus-rich, highly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained soil in deep shade; Oconee Bells is one of the rarest native wildflowers in North America and should only be sourced from specialty nurseries that propagate their own plants.

directions: Oconee Bells is one of the rarest and most celebrated native wildflowers in North America — an Appalachian endemic once thought extinct, restricted to a handful of gorge sites along the headwaters of the Savannah River in western South Carolina, southwestern North Carolina, and extreme northeastern Georgia, where it grows in spray zones of stream gorges in dense, deeply shaded, moist, acidic forests with rhododendron and galax as companions. A compact, slowly spreading evergreen ground cover with rounded, glossy, galax-like leaves and nodding, fringed white to pale pink bell-shaped flowers that emerge from January to March before most woodland plants have awoken, it is one of the first signs of spring in the southern mountain garden. It requires extremely acidic, consistently moist, humus-rich soil with perfect drainage in deep shade, and is notoriously slow to establish — expect 2–5 years before a planting spreads noticeably. Source only from specialty native plant nurseries; wild collection is illegal and has historically devastated native populations.

planting.note: Divide clumps in fall, or plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. Large-flowered Bellwort spreads slowly by rhizome to form attractive ground-cover colonies in shaded woodland gardens.

directions: Large-flowered Bellwort is a graceful native spring woodland herb, native to rich, moist deciduous forests from Quebec and Ontario south to Alabama and Georgia, blooming in April and May with pendant, bright yellow, twisted, bell-shaped flowers that droop elegantly from arching stems before the leaves have fully expanded. The leaves are perfoliate — the stem appears to pierce through the center of each leaf — giving the plant an unusual architectural quality even out of bloom, and the plant spreads slowly by rhizome to form attractive colonies excellent as a shade ground cover. Divide clumps in fall or purchase nursery-grown transplants; provide consistently moist, humus-rich soil amended with compost and mulched deeply with leaf litter, and the plants will slowly naturalize to fill in large shaded areas with minimal care.

planting.note: Divide clumps in fall and plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. Sessile-leaf Bellwort tolerates somewhat drier conditions than Uvularia grandiflora and adapts well to a wider range of shade garden soils.

directions: Sessile-leaf Bellwort is a delicate native woodland wildflower, native to moist to mesic deciduous forests and forest edges from New Brunswick and Manitoba south to Georgia and Oklahoma, spreading slowly by rhizome to form loose, airy colonies in the shade garden. Smaller and more delicate than Large-flowered Bellwort (Uvularia grandiflora), it produces pale cream-yellow, narrowly bell-shaped pendant flowers in April and May on slender, branching stems; the leaves are sessile (non-clasping) rather than perfoliate, distinguishing this species from its cousin. An easy-going, adaptable native wildflower that tolerates slightly drier soil than U. grandiflora, Sessile-leaf Bellwort is a good choice for gardeners working with thinner, drier woodland soils where other spring wildflowers struggle. Divide clumps in fall, plant in humus-rich soil, mulch with leaf litter, and allow colonies to naturalize.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade. Swamp Dogwood thrives along stream banks, pond margins, wet meadow edges, and seasonally flooded areas; it is excellent for rain gardens and bio-retention swales where the soil remains consistently moist.

directions: Swamp Dogwood is a large, multi-stemmed native shrub native to wet thickets, streambanks, pond margins, and swamp edges throughout eastern North America from New Brunswick to Florida and west to Oklahoma, tolerating prolonged seasonal flooding and thriving in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade. It produces flat-topped clusters of small white flowers in June and July that attract a wide variety of native bees and other pollinators, followed by striking porcelain-blue fruits on red-stemmed clusters that ripen in August and September and are eagerly consumed by over 40 species of songbirds, including wood thrush, catbirds, and warblers. The purplish-red winter stems provide attractive four-season interest, and the shrub suckers readily to form dense thickets that stabilize streambanks and provide excellent nesting cover for wildlife. An outstanding plant for wet native gardens, rain gardens, or bio-swales where its moisture tolerance is an asset rather than a compromise.
